coredata.dc:title: Simple method for visual detection of nitrite using fluorescence and colorimetry by poly (tannic acid) nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Xiaohui
Shen, Ao
Li, Mengwen
Duan, Ruochen
Hou, Lala
Zhao, Xiuqing
Li, Ziqi
Zhao, Yongwei
Zhang, Panqing
Wang, Xuebing
Li, Xue
Yang, Yunxu
coredata.dc:description: The nitration reaction of nitrite and phenolic substances was first used to identify and detect NO2 − by taking fluorescent poly (tannic acid) nanoparticles (FPTA NPs) as sensing platform. With the low cost, good biodegradable and convenient water-soluble FPTA NPs, a fluorescent and colorimetric dual modes detecting assay was realized. In fluorescent mode, the linear detection range of NO2 − was 0–36 μM, the LOD was as low as 3.03 nM, and the response time was 90 s. In colorimetric mode, the linear detection range of NO2 − was 0–46 μM, and the LOD was as low as 27 nM. Besides, a smartphone with FPTA NPs@ agarose hydrogel formed a portable detection platform to test the fluorescent and visible color changes of FPTA NPs for NO2 − sensing as well as for accurate visualization and quantitative detection of NO2 − in actual water and food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrite
Fluorescent poly (tannic acid) nanoparticles
Dual-mode detection
Fluorescence
Colorimetry

Preparation of FPTA NPs@ agarose hydrogel and the fluorescence sensing color (RGB value) tests for NO2 − with smartphone
Optimization of conditions for NO2 − sensing system
Detection of NO2 − based on the fluorescence and UV–vis spectrum signals
Selectivity of FPTA NPs to NO2 − in solution
Mechanism for detection of NO2 − via FPTA NPs
Detection of NO2 − based on FPTA NPs@ agarose hydrogel platform with smartphone
Detection of real samples by FPTA NPs@ agarose hydrogel platform with smartphone

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM image of FPTA NPs, Insert: Particle size distribution of FPTA NPs; (b) FT-IR spectrum of tannic acid, FPTA NPs and FPTA NPs in the presence of NO2 −. of NO2 − were studied. As could be seen from Fig. 2a, the fluorescent intensity of FPTA.

Optimization of pH value for detecting NO2 − by FPTA NPs: (a) Fluorescent intensities of FPTA NPs (0.2 mg mL−1) in aqueous solutions at different pH values; (b) The F/F
0 in different pH before and after the addition of NO2 − (λex = 349 nm, λem = 445 nm).

The fluorescence response time of FPTA NPs (0.2 mg mL−1, pH = 3) to NO2 − upon addition of NO2 − (200 μM) at 25 °C. Insert: The fluorescence color changes of FPTA NPs in the absence and presence of NO2 − (λex = 349 nm, λem = 445 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence spectra of FPTA NPs upon addition of different concentrations of NO2 −; (b) Plot of F/F
0 versus NO2 − concentration. Inset: Linear relationship between the fluorescent intensity ratio F/F
0 and NO2 − concentration; (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of FPTA NPs upon incremental addition of NO2 −; (d) Plot of absorption spectra of FPTA NPs versus NO2 − concentration and the linear relationship between the fluorescent intensity ratio F/F
0 and NO2 − concentration.

Relative value (fluorescent intensity ratio of F/F
0 , λex = 349 nm, λem = 445 nm and UV–vis absorption ratio of A/A
0 at 354 nm) of FPTA NPs (0.2 mg mL−1) in the presence of relative metal ions (1.0 mM) or anions (1.0 mM) before and after the addition of NO2 − (150 μM) in pH = 3.
The UV–vis absorption of FPTA NPs and FPTA NPs with NO2 −. Inset: the photos of the FPTA NPs (left) and FPTA NPs with NO2 − (right) under daylight.

(a) SEM images of FPTA NPs in presence of NO2 −. (b-d) EDS and elements mapping of FPTA NPs in presence of NO2 −. (b) C; (c) N; (d) O; (e) The result of elemental analysis of FPTA NPs in presence of NO2 − obtained from EDS spectra.
XPS spectra of FPTA NPs in presence of NO2 −. (a) Full scan spectrum, (b) High resolution spectra of C 1s, (c) N 1s and (d) O 1s.

(a) CIE coordinate of FPTA NPs (B) and FPTA NPs in presence of NO2 − (C); (b) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of FPTA NPs (0.20 mg mL−1) and FPTA NPs in presence of NO2 − (200 μM), respectively (pH = 3, and kex = 375 nm).

(a) Schematic diagram of FPTA NPs@ agarose hydrogel combined with a smartphone platform to detect NO2 − in dual-mode. (b) Photos of FPTA NPs@ agarose hydrogel under UV lamp and daylight to detect NO2 −. (c) The linear relationship between B values and NO2 − (0–36 μM) in fluorescence mode. (d) The linear relationship between G + B values and NO2 − (0–46 μM) in colorimetric mode.
Schematic illustration of synthesis and application of FPTA NPs for optical dual-mode detection of NO2 −.
Schematic illustration of fluorescent quenching mechanism of FPTA NPs in the presence of NO2 − solution (pH = 3).
Analysis of nitrite by dual modes in real samples.

head.ce:title: Simple method for visual detection of nitrite using fluorescence and colorimetry by poly (tannic acid) nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, School of Chemistry and Biological Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing, 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
School of Chemistry and Biological Engineering
University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, School of Chemistry and Biological Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing, 100083, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nitration reaction of nitrite and phenolic substances was first used to identify and detect NO2 − by taking fluorescent poly (tannic acid) nanoparticles (FPTA NPs) as sensing platform. With the low cost, good biodegradable and convenient water-soluble FPTA NPs, a fluorescent and colorimetric dual modes detecting assay was realized. In fluorescent mode, the linear detection range of NO2 − was 0–36 μM, the LOD was as low as 3.03 nM, and the response time was 90 s. In colorimetric mode, the linear detection range of NO2 − was 0–46 μM, and the LOD was as low as 27 nM. Besides, a smartphone with FPTA NPs@ agarose hydrogel formed a portable detection platform to test the fluorescent and visible color changes of FPTA NPs for NO2 − sensing as well as for accurate visualization and quantitative detection of NO2 − in actual water and food samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite (NO2 −) ion is ubiquitous in environment, food and physiological systems, which is an important intermediate product in nitrogen cycle [1,2]. NO2 − can interfere with the oxygen transport system in body, resulting in severe impairment of oxygen exchange ability of hemoglobin in blood, methemoglobin syndrome, which can lead to hypoxia poisoning or even asphyxia [3,4]. NO2 − can also react with secondary amines or amides in the stomach to form carcinogenic nitrosamines [5]. Therefore, to develop a sensitive, rapid and precise determination method for NO2 − is necessary.

At present, the detection of NO2 − is mainly based on diazotization reaction [6–9], which consists of two main types of reactions, Griess reaction and the use of o-phenylenediamine reaction to produce benzotriazole. As a standard method, Griess test refers to the reaction of NO2 − with sulfonamide to produce diazonium salt, which is often coupled with naphthalene ethylenediamine hydrochloride to produce fuchsin dye as an indicator of NO2 − [10]. Although with low detection limit, Griess method has interference by sample color which hinders its application in real-time detection [11,12]. In addition, fluorophore containing aromatic o-diamine, often as a reactive probe, can react with NO2 − to form fluorescent benzotriazole, in the process mostly using photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism to fluorescent molecule switch [13,14]. However, most developed o-diamine-based probes often suffered from long response time, poor water solubility or tedious synthesis steps [15], which limited their further development. Therefore, it is urgent to explore new detection mechanisms of NO2 −. Nitration is a possible mechanism to detect nitrite selectively and sensitively [16]. Meanwhile, full of interesting reports have shown that •NO2 produced by nitrous acid in aqueous solution can combine with phenol to form nitrophenol specifically and sensitively [17–24], which is expected to the detection of NO2 −. Furthermore, a large number of fluorescent polymer nanoparticles have become promising materials due to their amazing stability, non-toxic properties and good surface properties [25], in particular, unlabeled fluorescent polymers from nature are attractive. For example, tannic acid (TA), a water-soluble polyphenol compound, can be as a precursor to form fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, which has great potential in sensing [26–28].

Inspired by the above information, herein, we designed and built a fluorescent and colorimetric dual mode detecting assay for NO2 − based on the specific and sensitive response between NO2 − and phenolic substances, by which fluorescent poly (tannic acid) nanoparticles (FPTA NPs) was used as sensing platform to detect NO2 −. In brief, Tannic acid (TA) was self-polymerized in ethanol solution and degraded by H2O2, which produced a low cost, good biodegradable and convenient water-soluble FPTA NPs. With the increased concentration of NO2 −, visible color changed from colorless to yellow, and blue fluorescence quench was achieved by FPTA NPs (Scheme 1 ). The dual output signals had wide linear relationship ranges with the NO2 − concentrations. In addition, FPTA NPs@ agarose hydrogel platform with smartphone had generated and realized the two-mode detection of NO2 − in tap water and food real-samples successfully in real time. To the best of our knowledge, there was no research on detecting NO2 − by the dual signals of this water-soluble polyphenol nano-polymer so far. The results should enrich the fluorescent and visible double signal detection assay a little now [29–37].
Materials and Instrumentation are provided in SI.

The synthesis of fluorescent poly (tannic acid) nanoparticles (FPTA NPs) was based on previous reported literature [26]. In short, 0.1 g of tannic acid (TA) and NaOH (20 mg) were added to anhydrous ethanol (5 mL) under ultrasonic condition for 30 min. Then 2.0 mL of H2O2 (30%) was added to the mixed solution to reflux for 2 h. Centrifuge, lyophilize and the yellow solid was obtained. Finally, the as-prepared FPTA NPs were stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimization condition for the test processes
ce:section.ce:para:
0.1 mL FPTA NPs solution (20 mg mL−1) was mixed evenly with 9.8 mL buffer solution (pH = 1–7), and 0.1 mL NO2 − standard solution (1500 μM) was added to mix evenly. The buffer solution was prepared with dilute hydrochloric acid and dilute sodium hydroxide solutions into different pH solutions. The fluorescence F
0 and F were measured in different pH solutions, where F
0 and F denoted the fluorescent intensity of FPTA NPs in the absence and presence of NO2 −, and all experiments were conducted at room temperature. All fluorescence spectra were recorded in a range from 340 nm to 700 nm with excitation at 349 nm. The excitation and emission bandwidths were 5.0 nm and 10.0 nm separately.

The mixture containing FPTA NPs solution (0.1 mL, 20 mg mL−1) and 9.8 mL buffer solution (pH = 3) was prepared, and NO2 − standard solution (0.1 mL, 1500 μM) was added to mix evenly. Then the fluorescence spectra were recorded at some time interval. The time when the fluorescence intensity did not change was used as the index to determine the optimal reaction time.

FPTA NPs solution (0.1 mL, 20 mg mL−1) was mixed evenly with 9.8 mL buffer solution (pH = 3), and 0.1 mL standard solution of NO2 − in various concentrations was added to mix evenly. UV–vis and fluorescent spectrum measurements were measured. All UV-spectra were recorded in a range from 250 nm to 450 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of FPTA NPs@ agarose hydrogel and the fluorescence sensing color (RGB value) tests for NO2 − with smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: First, 0.2 g agarose was added to 9 mL deionized water, heated by microwave to dissolve and cooled to 50 °C. FPTA NPs solution (1 mL, 20 mg mL−1) was added into the above solution, stirring evenly to obtain 2.0% mixed agarose solution. The mixed solution (250 μL) was added to vitric sample sink and then naturally cooled down to room temperature. Then different concentrations of NO2 − solution (pH = 3) to be measured were added into the sample sink. Finally, the hydrogel was analyzed by fluorescence and visual colorimetry using a smart phone once the color of the hydrogel was uniform.

Herein, the experimental samples of bread and sausage were bought from the local supermarket in Beijing, the tap water was also obtained from the municipal water of Beijing. The bread and sausage were mixed with equal amount of water and homogenized well in a tissue masher. Then, the sample homogenate (5 g) and 80 mL water were added into 150 mL conical bottle, ultrasonic extraction was carried out for 30 min, then heated to 75 °C and maintained for 5 min. Then transfer quantitatively to 100 mL volumetric bottle, dilute to scale with water and mix well. The above spare solution (15 mL) was filtered it through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and then diluted to a certain volume with ultra-pure water. The solution after sample treatment was collected and immediately used for subsequent determination experiments. The recovery rate was calculated by the standard addition method.

The morphologies of the prepared FPTA NPs were characterized by the following tests. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) showed that FPTA NPs existed in the form of tight spherical structure with good dispersion. The diameter distribution diagram showed that the size distribution range of FPTA NPs was 1.5–6.5 nm (Fig. 1 a).

FT-IR spectra of TA and FPTA NPs could be seen in Fig. 1b, and the characteristic absorption peaks of both were much similar even if there were slightly different [26], which contained the characteristic absorption peaks of O–H, C=O, benzene skeleton and C–O. The absorption bands between 3000 and 3600 cm−1 were due to the stretching vibrations of O–H of TA and polyphenols respectively. The absorption peak at 1713 cm−1 was corresponded to the C=O vibration, 1605 cm−1 and 1455 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of benzene skeleton, while C–O vibration of TA and polyphenols appeared at 1357 cm−1 and 1217 cm−1, respectively. At the same time, the elemental content of the nanoparticles was determined by elemental analysis (Fig. S1), and the proportion of C and O elements were 68.02% and 31.98%, respectively. While, MALDI-TOF-MS analysis of FPTA NPs had been tested and also reflected the existence of the fragment peaks of FPTA NPs in the m/z range of 137–1730 (Fig. S2). The photobleaching resistance and salt tolerance of FPTA NPs were investigated. As shown in Fig. S3a, the fluorescent intensity of FPTA NPs at 445 nm basically remained stable after 48 h of continuous irradiation with the excitation light at 349 nm. Besides, the fluorescent intensity of FPTA NPs almost unchanged under high ionic strengths as shown in Fig. S3b. These indicated that FPTA NPs had outstanding optical stability, which had potential application prospect in sensing field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of conditions for NO2 − sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the optimal pH for detecting NO2 − by FPTA NPs, the fluorescent intensity changes of FPTA NPs in different pH value and before and after the addition

NPs solution increased with incremental pH value, meanwhile reached to the top at pH = 3, and then decreased gradually. As could be seen from Fig. 2b, the ratio of fluorescent intensity before and after the addition of NO2 − were taken, and F/F
0 was the minimum when pH = 2, which meant that the fluorescent quenching efficiency was the highest (F
0 indicated the initial fluorescent intensity, F as the fluorescent intensity after the addition of NO2 − to the detection system). However, considering the fluorescent intensity of the initial FPTA NPs solution, pH = 3 was chosed as the optimal pH value in the following detecting experiments. Furthermore, it was also verified that FPTA NPs solution had good optical stability and salt tolerance at pH = 3 under the same experimental conditions (Fig. S3).

To determine the response time of FPTA NPs to NO2 −, NO2 − (200 μM) was added to the FPTA NPs solution in pH = 3. Mix it evenly, and measure the fluorescence at a certain interval (Fig. 3
). It could be seen that the fluorescent intensity of the system decreased continuously, and no longer changed until 90 s. Moreover, after adding 150 μM NO2 −.

it could be clearly seen that the solution had a blue fluorescence quench under 365 nm lamp. While, when the temperature gradually increased from 25 °C to 50 °C, the fluorescent intensity of FPTA NPs and FPTA NPs with NO2 − were basically stable (Fig. S4), which proved that the detecting system based on FPTA NPs had good thermal stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of NO2 − based on the fluorescence and UV–vis spectrum signals
ce:section.ce:para:
To explore the sensitivity of FPTA NPs to NO2 −, titration experiments of fluorescent emission spectrum and UV–vis spectrum were performed. As presented in Fig. 4 a, it revealed that the fluorescent intensity at 445 nm decreased with the addition of NO2 − and had a blue shift. An excellent linear relationship of F/F
0 with NO2 − in 0–36 μM could be obtained (F/F
0 = -0.0185C + 0.9903, R
2 = 0.9970, Fig. 4b). Wherein, F
0 and F were the fluorescent intensity of FPTA NPs without and with NO2 − at 445 nm, and C was the NO2 − concentration, LOD was 3.03 nM. Meanwhile, the absorbance of FPTA NPs at 350 nm increased with the incremental concentrations of NO2 − and were linearly fitted with NO2 − concentrations (Fig. 4c). A good linear relationship in 0–48 μM could be obtained (Fig. 4d). The regression equation was A = 0.0107C + 0.1146, and the correlation coefficient R
2 was 0.9994. LOD was 27 nM (3σ).

To prove the advantages of this method, we compared it with other reported methods as shown in Table S1, and it could be concluded that the LOD and response time in our work had great advantages. Moreover, in the process of dual-mode detection of NO2 −, colorimetric method had excellent visualization effect without using any instrument, which was relatively intuitive. And the fluorescence method was highly sensitive and could eliminate the interference from sample color on the test. The dual-mode sensor can avoid false positive results by dual-signal output. Therefore, this sensing platform was suitable for detection NO2 − in a variety of complex environments, which had the potential to detect NO2 − in real time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of FPTA NPs to NO2 − in solution
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of FPTA NPs to NO2 − was studied by fluorescence emission spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra. Selectivity and anti-interference experiments of FPTA NPs (2 mg mL−1) showed good selectivity to NO2 − at pH = 3, and were almost free of interference from relative cations (K+, Na+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Ba2+and Co2+) and relative anions (I−, F−, Cl−, Br−, CO3 2-, NO3 −, HS−, ClO4 −, SO4 2-, H2PO4 −, HCO3 − and HPO4 2-), which displayed selectivity and high anti-interference performance (Fig. 5 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for detection of NO2 − via FPTA NPs
ce:section.ce:para:
Literature reported that nitrous acid (HNO2) can produce nitrogen dioxide free radical (•NO2) which was a powerful nitrification agent of aromatic compounds in atmospheric water [38–40] as displayed in equation (1), and can selectively react with phenolic substances as a medium oxidant (•NO2, E
0 = 1.03 V, relative to standard hydrogen electrode) to produce nitroaromatic compounds as shown on equation (2)-(4) [41,42]. In addition, the ortho- and para-sites of the hydroxyl group in phenolic substances were the possible attack sites for the free radical •NO2, and with the passivating effect on the nitroaromatic reactivity by electron drawing effect of nitro, mononitration product would prevent the generation of multiple nitro [23].
So, to further verify the detection mechanism of NO2 − via FPTA NPs, UV–vis, FT-IR, EDS, XPS and fluorescent lifetime were further studied as shown below.

SEM, EDS and elements mapping were used to characterize FPTA NPs in presence of NO2 − as shown in Fig. 7 . As could be seen from Fig. 7a, it had displayed macroporous structure after the recognized reaction of FPTA NPs with NO2 −, and compared with FPTA NPs, the composition and proportion of elements (C, O, N) in nitrification recognizing product also had obvious change (Fig. 7b–d). The atomic percentage of C elements in FPTA NPs with NO2 − was now calculated to be 62.15%, which was lower than that of the original ratio in FPTA NPs (68.02%), and the atomic percentages of O and N elements increased from 31.98% to 0–34.58% and 3.27% respectively, which was consistent with the assumption that the nitration reaction took place in FPTA NPs after detecting NO2 − (Fig. 7e).

In addition, the recognition products of FPTA NPs with NO2 − were separated and studied by XPS spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 8 . The element state and chemical composition verified the coexistence of C, O as well as N elements in the recognition products. High-resolution of C 1s (Fig. 8b) spectra showed the presence of C–C or C=C (284.68 eV), C–N or C–O (286.18 eV) and C=O (288.38 eV) [45]. As shown in Fig. 8c, the spectrum of N 1s had three peaks of C–N, N–O and NO2, which were located at 399.71 eV, 401.68 eV and 406.69 eV respectively, indicating the formation of nitroaromatic compounds. And the spectral peaks of O 1s appeared at 531.58 eV, 532.38 eV and 533.28 eV, which were attributed to the C–O or N–O, O–H and C=O bonds respectively (Fig. 8d).

We converted the fluorescence spectra before and after the identification and detection of NO2 − by FPTA NPs into three-color coordinates. It could be seen from Fig. 9 a that after the identification of NO2 −, the Commission Internationale de I'Eclairage (CIE) coordinates changed from B (0.17, 0.14) to C (0.25, 0.32), which verified the change of fluorescence color. Moreover, the fluorescent lifetime of FPTA NPs and the identified product had also been tested to further verify the fluorescent quenching mechanism. As could be seen from the fluorescent lifetime curve in Fig. 9b, the lifetimes of FPTA NPs shortened from 4.86 ns to 3.83 ns in the presence of NO2 −, which confirmed that a dynamic quenching process should have occurred [46].

Based on the evidence had been presented, FPTA NPs could undoubtedly detect NO2 − through the interaction triggered by •NO2 and phenoxyl radical under acidic conditions during the recognition (Scheme 2 ). Once NO2 − was added, the radical substitution reaction occurred, and then quenched by water to form mononitration ortho-nitrated phenol derivatives. Consequently, a blue fluorescence quenched under 365 nm lamp and showed out a visible color change from colorless to deep yellow due to the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) [47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of NO2 − based on FPTA NPs@ agarose hydrogel platform with smartphone
ce:section.ce:para:
A portable detection platform that combined with smartphone and color sensitive FPTA NPs@ agarose hydrogel was generated. So, upon addition with different concentrations of NO2 − to the sample sink, clearly visible color changes of the hydrogel displayed from colorless to yellow (Fig. 10 a), and from blue fluorescence to quench under the UV lamp (Fig. 10b). The smartphone color recognition APP would then output the corresponding RGB values by recording the color of FPTA NPs@ agarose hydrogel and analyzing the colors of the sample. Intriguingly, the B value, in the fluorescent mode, had a good linear relationship with the concentration of NO2 − from 0 to 36 μM, and R
2 was 0.9977 (Fig. 10c). While, the G + B value, in colorimetric mode, had a good linear relationship with the concentration of NO2 − from 0 to 46 μM, and R
2 was 0.9996 (Fig. 10d). Thus, NO2 − could be detected expediently in real time via fluorescent and colorimetric dual modes with portable smartphone system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of real samples by FPTA NPs@ agarose hydrogel platform with smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further verify the practicality of the portable smartphone system of FPTA NPs@ agarose hydrogel, three kinds real samples (including tap water in Beijing, bread and sausage) were selected for standard addition and recovery experiments, and NO2 − was detected by the hydrogel system in dual modes. NO2 − solutions (5, 10 and 30 μM) were spiked into the above samples for further analysis. It could be seen from Table 1 that the recovery rate was ranged from 97.9% to 104.0%, and the reliability and practicability of FPTA NPs@ agarose hydrogel for detecting NO2 − were verified. The FPTA NPs@ agarose hydrogel showed good sensitivity and accuracy to detect NO2 − for actual samples.

In summary, a fluorescent poly (tannic acid) nanoparticles of FPTA NPs were synthesized, and the fluorescent and colorimetric dual mode identification of NO2 − was first realized by the nitration reaction between FPTA NPs and NO2 − with the advantages of simple preparation, good specificity and high sensitivity. While, a smartphone and the FPTA NPs@ agarose hydrogel had formed a portable detection platform to test the fluorescent and visible color changes of FPTA NPs for NO2 − sensing as well as for accurate visualization and quantitative detection of NO2 − in actual water and food samples. What's more, the detection results from portable platform maintained consistency with the above fluorescence and colorimetry. Therefore, the results showed that the promising recognizing mechanism of FPTA NPs to NO2 − exhibited a certain reliability and practicability in detecting or assessing of NO2 − in water samples and food products.

Xiaohui Hao: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Ao Shen: Validation. Mengwen Li: Investigation, Formal analysis. Ruochen Duan: Conceptualization. Lala Hou: Project administration. Xiuqing Zhao: Formal analysis. Ziqi Li: Software. Xue Li: Visualization. Yunxu Yang: Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (No. 21575012), the Natural Science Foundation of Beijing (No. 2212010), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (FRF-BR-19-003B).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2023.341280.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of nitrate and nitrite in different seafood

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Chiesa, F. Arioli, R. Pavlovic, R. Villa, S. Panseri, Detection of nitrate and nitrite in different seafood, Food Chem. 288 (2019) 361-367.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrate and nitrites in foods: worldwide regional distribution in view of their risks and benefits

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Kalaycioglu, F. B. Erim, Nitrate and nitrites in foods: Worldwide regional distribution in view of their risks and benefits, J. Agric. Food Chem. 67 (2019) 7205-7222.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrite in feed: from animal health to human health

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Cockburn, G. Brambilla, M.L. Fernandez, D. Arcella, L. R. Bordajandi, B. Cottrill, C. V. Peteghem, J. L. Dorne, Nitrite in feed: from animal health to human health, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 270 (2013) 209-217.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Olive-structured” nanocomposite based on multiwalled carbon nanotubes decorated with an electroactive copolymer for environmental nitrite detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Zhao, H. Yang, S. Xu, X. Li, W. Wei, X. Liu, “Olive-structured” nanocomposite based on multiwalled carbon nanotubes decorated with an electroactive copolymer for environmental nitrite detection, ACS Sustain. Chem. Eng. 7 (2019) 17424-17431.
D. Forman, S. Al-Dabbagh, R. Doll, Nitrate and gastric cancer risks, Nature 317 (1985) 676.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel highly specific colorimetric fluorescent probe for the detection of nitrite in aqueous solution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Xu, Y. Shi, G. Xu, Q. Zhao, L. Hui, S. Zhang, B. Zhu, Z. Xu, Z. Bian, A novel highly specific colorimetric fluorescent probe for the detection of nitrite in aqueous solution, Luminescence 37 (2022) 729-733.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel ratiometric and colorimetric probe for rapid and ultrasensitive detection of nitrite in water based on an Acenaphtho [1,2-d] imidazole derivative

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.D. Yilmaz, A novel ratiometric and colorimetric probe for rapid and ultrasensitive detection of nitrite in water based on an Acenaphtho [1,2-d] imidazole derivative, Anal. Chim. Acta 1166 (2021) 338597.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on spectroscopic methods for determination of nitrite and nitrate in environmental samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Singh, Y.R. Beg, G.R. Nishad, A review on spectroscopic methods for determination of nitrite and nitrate in environmental samples, Talanta 191 (2019) 364-381.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fast and sensitive fluorescent detection of nitrite based on an amino-functionalized MOFs of UiO-66-NH2
